meta.label: 🌋 Iceland
meta.sub: KEFLAVÍK → GOLDEN CIRCLE → SOUTH COAST → EAST FJORDS → MÝVATN → SNÆFELLSNES → KEFLAVÍK · 20 – 29 AUG 2026
meta.intro: Tap any card to open the day — each one gives you the essential stops, a splurge-vs-budget call on the one big activity, and the practical van-life detail that actually matters. Prices are per couple in ISK; swap the toggle to see the South & West cut if you'd rather trade the East Fjords and Mývatn for a more relaxed pace.

startCard.title: Pick up the van — Keflavík
startCard.meta: THU 20 AUG · 11:00 collection · Iðavellir 11 · stock up at Bónus, fuel up

endCard.title: Drop off the van — Keflavík
endCard.meta: SAT 29 AUG · 09:30 return · refuel near KEF, leave 90 min buffer

presets.label: 🌋 Full Ring Road
presets.desc: All 9 nights — the complete anti-clockwise loop.

presets.label: 🌊 South & West
presets.desc: Skip the long east/north legs — Golden Circle, South Coast to Jökulsárlón, then Snæfellsnes.

days.note: Land, grab the van at KEF, load up at Bónus, then ease into Iceland with an evening dip — the geothermal scene starts now.

badges.t: Pre-book Sky Lagoon online — sells out
Buy groceries at Bónus near KEF today

why: Van pickup and first feel of the open road — the Ring Road starts here
Sky Lagoon's infinity edge merges with the Atlantic horizon at golden hour
Bónus supermarket run sets you up to cook cheaply all week
tips: Iðavellir 11 is 5 min from KEF arrivals — pick up at 11:00, ask for a van orientation walk-through and test the gas burner
Late August sun sets around 21:30; Sky Lagoon is magical 19:00–21:00 — book the Sér ritual package if budget allows
days.cost: Van incl. · Sky Lagoon ~6,990 ISK pp (Pure) or 11,490 ISK pp (Sér ritual) · Bónus shop ~3,000–5,000 ISK

stops.d: Collect the campervan at Iðavellir 11, 230 Keflavík. Do a full walk-around, test the gas stove and heating, and fill a water jug at the depot before rolling.
Stock up here — Bónus is Iceland's cheapest chain. Load pasta, oats, eggs, cheese, canned fish, fresh veg and coffee. Supermarket prices in Reykjavík proper are higher.
Infinity-edge geothermal pool jutting over the Atlantic, opened 2021. The seven-step Skjól ritual — steam, cold plunge, sauna, scrub, lagoon — is worth the upgrade. Towel rental available.
stops.sp: Sér ritual package (private changing + scrub) 11,490 ISK pp
stops.bu: Pure pass (no ritual) 6,990 ISK pp — still the same lagoon

stops.d: Walk the harbour front past colourful fishing boats to Harpa concert hall. Grab a hot dog from Bæjarins Beztu (Iceland's most famous street food, ~600 ISK) and watch the sunset light on the bay.

days.note: Iceland's greatest hits in one long day — tectonic rifts, erupting geysers and a thundering double waterfall, capped by a soak in the Secret Lagoon.

badges.t: Book Secret Lagoon online (sells out in summer)

why: Strokkur erupts every 5–10 minutes — you will get drenched if you stand downwind
Gullfoss two-tier plunge into a canyon is legitimately one of Iceland's most powerful sights
Þingvellir is where you literally stand between the Eurasian and North American tectonic plates
tips: Drive the loop anticlockwise (Þingvellir → Geysir → Gullfoss → Kerið → Secret Lagoon) to dodge coach buses at each stop
Secret Lagoon in Flúðir is far cheaper and less crowded than Blue Lagoon — save Blue Lagoon for the last night near KEF
days.cost: Þingvellir parking 750 ISK · Geysir free · Gullfoss free · Kerið 900 ISK pp · Secret Lagoon ~3,200 ISK pp · campsite ~2,500 ISK pp

stops.d: Walk the Almannagjá rift — a 7km canyon that IS the boundary between two tectonic plates. In late August the birches turn copper-gold. The Öxará river path to Lögberg (Law Rock) takes about 45 min and requires no special gear.
Strokkur hurls a column of boiling water 15–30m into the air every 5–8 minutes. Stand on the south side for the best spray-free view. The surrounding hot spring field is eerie and free to wander.
The Hvítá river drops 32m in two thunderous stages into a narrow canyon. Walk down to the lower viewing platform — you'll feel the spray from 50 m away. Late afternoon light turns the mist golden.

Iceland's oldest public pool (1891), in a farm village. Constant 38–40°C, natural geothermal source, small geyser that burps beside you in the pool. Far more authentic than the tourist lagoons — book ahead.
stops.sp: Blue Lagoon if you skip it today (save for Day 9)
stops.bu: Selfoss public swimming pool 1,100 ISK pp — hot pots, waterslide, locals only

days.note: Waterfall behind a waterfall, a glacier you can touch, puffins on black sea stacks, and the most dramatic beach in Iceland — all in one stunning drive to Vík.

badges.t: Book glacier hike guide at Sólheimajökull in advance
Vík campsites fill fast in August — arrive by 17:00

why: Seljalandsfoss lets you walk behind the curtain of water — wear waterproofs and embrace the soaking
Sólheimajökull glacier is accessible without a guide for the moraine viewpoint, but an ice-walk takes you onto the blue ice
Reynisfjara's stacked basalt columns, booming surf and puffin-topped sea stacks are unlike anywhere else on earth
tips: Seljalandsfoss is best in morning or evening light — the falls face west and glow amber in the afternoon
Sneaker waves at Reynisfjara beach have killed visitors — stay above the high-tide line and never turn your back on the ocean
days.cost: Seljalandsfoss parking 900 ISK · Skógafoss free · glacier hike ~12,900 ISK pp (Troll Expeditions or Arcanum) · Reynisfjara free · campsite ~2,000 ISK pp

stops.d: Walk the muddy path behind the main falls curtain — fully encircled by water and spray. Then scramble 200m north into a hidden canyon to find Gljúfrabúi, a waterfall sealed inside a cave. Wear waterproofs for both.

60m-wide falls drop 62m straight off the plateau edge. Climb the 527-step staircase beside the falls to the top for views of the coast and Eyjafjallajökull. The first kilometre of the Fimmvörðuháls trail continues to a viewpoint above — 20 min return and worth it.

A tongue of the Mýrdalsjökull ice cap creeps down to a lagoon — you can walk to the moraine edge for free or strap crampons on and walk the glacier with a guide. The ice is full of ash layers from the 2010 Eyjafjallajökull eruption. Book a 2-hour ice walk; departs from the car park.
stops.cost: Car park free · ice walk ~12,900 ISK pp
stops.sp: 3-hour glacier hike with ice climbing section ~17,900 ISK pp
stops.bu: Walk to the glacier lagoon viewpoint — free and still spectacular, 10 min from car park

stops.d: Jet-black volcanic sand, Reynisdrangar sea stacks (inhabited by puffins in August), and the Hálsanefshellir basalt column cave. Dyrhólaey arch viewpoint is 5 min drive east — the 120m sea arch and puffin cliffs at sunset are the end-of-day reward. Respect sneaker wave warnings.

days.note: Your most photogenic day — canyon, ice, and icebergs drifting past a black sand shore all before dinner.
why: Fjaðrárgljúfur — a cathedral canyon carved by a glacial flood, walls draped in moss
Skaftafell's Svínafellsjökull hike puts crampons on your feet and a glacier under them
Jökulsárlón icebergs calving into a tidal lagoon, then washing ashore as diamonds at sunset
tips: Guide-led glacier hike (3–4 h) must be booked ahead — Icelandic Mountain Guides or Local Guide; solo walking on glacier ice is prohibited
Diamond Beach light is best in the evening when the low August sun backlights the ice — time it for 19:00–21:00
days.cost: Fjaðrárgljúfur free · glacier hike ~6,500 ISK pp · lagoon boat ~7,500 ISK pp

stops.d: A 100 m deep, 2 km long basalt canyon sliced by glacial meltwater. The rim trail (30 min return) gives staggering top-down views of the ribbon river below. Go early before tour buses.
Strap on crampons and walk an actual outlet glacier — blue ice tunnels, crevasse edges, and panoramas of Vatnajökull. 3–4 h guided hike departing from the Skaftafell car park.
stops.sp: Private glacier guide + ice-climbing add-on ~15,000 ISK pp
stops.bu: Self-guided Skaftafell gorge + Svartifoss waterfall hike is free and stunning

stops.d: Milky-blue icebergs the size of houses drift past on their way to the sea. The zodiac or amphibian boat tour (~45 min) gets you right among them — seals often ride the bergs. Even watching from shore free is jaw-dropping.
stops.cost: Shore: free · Zodiac boat ~7,500 ISK pp
stops.sp: Amphibian boat tour (drives into lagoon) ~9,000 ISK pp
stops.bu: Walk the shore and watch seals from the bridge — 100% free

stops.d: Cross Route 1 from the lagoon and icebergs from Jökulsárlón litter the black sand like scattered crystal. Stay for the evening light — it's only 100 m from the car park, and it's the best photo of the trip.

days.note: A long winding drive rewards you with Iceland's quietest beauty — a dramatic mountain backdrop at dawn, wild reindeer in the highlands, and a rainbow street in a fairy-tale ferry village.
why: Vestrahorn's serrated black peaks reflected in a tidal lagoon — Iceland's most dramatic mountain portrait
The East Fjords road barely sees tourists; you'll have entire viewpoints to yourselves
Seyðisfjörður's painted rainbow road and tin-clad church feel like stumbling into a Wes Anderson set
tips: Stokksnes/Vestrahorn access road charges a small fee at a café hut (800 ISK pp) — it's worth every króna; arrive before 09:00 for calm reflections
Fill your tank in Höfn before leaving — fuel stations are sparse through the fjords; Djúpivogur has one
days.cost: Vestrahorn access ~800 ISK pp · most fjord stops free

stops.d: Jagged 454 m black peaks rise straight from a tidal sand spit. Walk the dune ridge at low tide for reflection shots — the mountain doubles itself perfectly in the shallow water. Wild, desolate, unforgettable.

A tiny fishing village with a fuel stop and a peculiar sculpture park: 34 giant stone eggs on the shoreline, each representing a local bird species. Stretch your legs, grab a coffee, and watch eider ducks potter around the harbour.

A 20 km detour down a dramatic mountain pass leads to Iceland's most charming town — a Norwegian-built fishing village of colourful tin houses at the end of a deep fjord. The rainbow road leads to the blue church. Reindeer sometimes graze the hillsides above town.
stops.cost: Free · dinner at Skaftfell Bistro ~3,500 ISK pp
stops.sp: Stay the night here instead of Egilsstaðir — Við Lónið guesthouse overlooks the fjord
stops.bu: Van pitch at the town campsite with fjord views ~2,800 ISK

stops.d: Pull over at the long black-sand shore of Iceland's third-largest lake on the way back into Egilsstaðir. Cook pasta in the van while loons call across the water. The lake is supposedly home to Iceland's own Loch Ness monster — Lagarfljótsormur.

days.note: Europe's most powerful waterfall before lunch, then an afternoon spent hopping between boiling mud pots, a secret warm cave, and a milky-blue geothermal lagoon — Iceland's geothermal heartland in a single day.
why: Dettifoss thunders 44 m into a grey gorge — 500 tonnes of glacial water per second, the most powerful falls in Europe
Hverir's boiling mud pots and sulphur vents look exactly like the surface of another planet
Grjótagjá's geothermally-warmed cave pool glows brilliant blue — you can wade in (water is ~50°C; bathe at the edges)
tips: Approach Dettifoss from the east bank (Route 862 gravel) for the most dramatic viewpoint and fewer people than the west side
Midge season at Mývatn peaks July–August — a buff or head net is genuinely useful at dusk; they don't bite but swarm thickly
days.cost: Dettifoss free · Mývatn Nature Baths ~7,000 ISK pp · Grjótagjá free

stops.d: A grey wall of glacial water 100 m wide crashes 44 m into the Jökulsárgljúfur canyon in a permanent rainbow of spray. The east-bank approach trail (15 min walk) puts you right at the lip. Bring a waterproof jacket — the mist soaks you from 50 m away.

Ochre and crimson hillside studded with boiling mud pools, hissing steam vents, and sulphur chimneys. The smell of rotten eggs hits you 200 m before you arrive. Walk the marked paths carefully — the crust is thin and the mud is genuinely boiling. No fee, no crowds.

A lava cave split by the mid-Atlantic rift holds a crystal-clear geothermally-heated pool glowing aquamarine. Famous as a filming location in Game of Thrones. You can wade in at the shallower edges (~42–48°C) — bliss after the drive. The cave is tiny and moody.
stops.bu: Free thermal bathing — a genuine hidden gem compared to the paid lagoons

stops.d: Silica-rich geothermal lagoon with volcano views and almost nobody from tour groups in the evenings. Less famous than the Blue Lagoon, far more authentic. Book the 20:00 slot for moody late light over the lake and a long quiet soak after a full day.
stops.sp: Add the private in-water steam tunnel experience
stops.bu: Grjótagjá cave (free, above) scratches the same itch

days.note: Your wildlife day — breaching humpbacks in Skjálfandi Bay, then harbour seals on the Vatnsnes peninsula, with Akureyri's surprising botanical garden squeezed in.

badges.t: Book whale watching NOW — Aug boats fill weeks out

why: Húsavík is Iceland's whale-watching capital — humpbacks feed in Skjálfandi Bay all summer and sightings are near-certain in late August
Vatnsnes peninsula hosts one of Iceland's densest harbour seal colonies at Hvítserkur; the basalt sea stack itself is eerily photogenic at dusk
Akureyri punches above its size: colourful timber houses, a free botanical garden at 66°N, and the best coffee outside Reykjavík
tips: North Sailing or Gentle Giants both run 3-hour whale tours; book the morning departure (09:00) so you have the afternoon for Vatnsnes — the 130 km detour is worth it
Vatnsnes road (Route 711) is a 60 km gravel loop — fine for 2WD campervans but slow; allow 2 hours including seal stops at Illugastaðir and the Hvítserkur viewpoint
days.cost: Whale tour ~6,900 ISK pp · Vatnsnes free · Botanical garden free · campsite ~4,500 ISK

stops.d: 3-hour boat tour into Skjálfandi Bay where humpbacks, minkes and white-beaked dolphins feed. Late August is peak season — multiple whales per trip is the norm, not the exception.
stops.sp: Gentle Giants oak schooner (silent sailing) ~9,900 ISK pp — worth every króna
stops.bu: Húsavík Whale Museum on the harbour (~2,200 ISK) if weather scrubs the boat

stops.d: A 30-metre horseshoe of water where the Skjálfandafljót river drops in a wide arc. Legend says the old law-speaker threw his Norse idols in here when Iceland converted to Christianity in AD 1000. Both banks are accessible; the west side viewpoint is best.

A 15-metre basalt stack rising from the sea that looks uncannily like a drinking dragon. Walk down to Illugastaðir farm beach beforehand — harbour seals laze on the rocks here year-round, unbothered by visitors who keep a respectful distance.

The world's northernmost botanic garden is absurdly lush in August — roses, arctic poppies, even bananas. Walk down Hafnarstraeti for a coffee at Bláa Kannan, then spot the heart-shaped traffic lights that are Akureyri's signature. Free parking on the harbour.

days.note: Iceland's 'miniature' in one day: a glacier volcano, lava-crunch beaches, sea-cave arches, and the cone that Jules Verne sent his heroes into the earth through.
why: Kirkjufell is Iceland's most-photographed mountain — the double waterfall foreground at dawn or dusk is genuinely one of the great landscape shots on earth
Snæfellsjökull glacier caps a dormant stratovolcano at the peninsula's tip; on a clear late-August day you can see Reykjavík, 120 km away
Djúpalónssandur is a black pebble beach strewn with shipwreck iron and lifting stones — tactile, eerie and completely free
tips: It's a 4–5 hour drive from Akureyri/Varmahlíð: leave by 07:30 to have a full afternoon on the peninsula. Route 54 west along the south shore is faster; save the north shore for the return crawl to Borgarnes

Snæfellsjökull glacier hike requires a guide — Summit Adventures runs small-group glacier walks (~10,000 ISK pp, 3 h); book online or call on approach. If skipping the guided glacier, the Arnarstapi–Hellnar coastal walk (2.5 km each way) is superb and completely free
days.cost: Kirkjufell free · Glacier hike ~10,000 ISK pp · Djúpalónssandur free · campsite ~4,500 ISK

stops.d: Iceland's iconic arrowhead mountain with the multi-strand Kirkjufellsfoss waterfall in the foreground. The classic shot is from the east bank footbridge at blue hour — in late August that means around 22:00. Park in the signed car park on Route 54 in Grundarfjörður.

A 2.5 km path between two tiny fishing villages along raw lava cliffs riddled with sea arches, blowholes and Arctic tern colonies. August terns still dive-bomb walkers — a hat helps. Café Hellnar at the far end does the best fish soup on the peninsula.

Crampons on, guide leading, you cross the ice cap of the glacier Jules Verne used as his gateway to the centre of the earth. The 360° view from the rim on a clear day takes in Reykjavík, the Westfjords, and the whole peninsula below.
stops.sp: Summit Adventures 'Glacier & Crater' full-day ~18,000 ISK pp
stops.bu: Drive to the Snæfellsjökull National Park visitor centre at Malarif lighthouse — free views across the ice, no gear needed

stops.d: A secluded cove of jet-black pebbles scattered with rusted iron from a British trawler wrecked in 1948 — touching the wreck pieces is prohibited but haunting to walk among. Four lifting stones ('strength stones') test the old fishermen's fitness scale: Fullsterkur (full strong) at 154 kg down to Amlóði (useless) at 23 kg.

days.short: Reykjanes — Blue Lagoon, Tectonic Ridge & Last Night
days.note: The Ring Road ends where it began — on a volcanic peninsula that smells of sulphur and holds both the world's most famous geothermal spa and the exact crack between two tectonic plates.

badges.t: Pre-book Blue Lagoon slot ASAP — sell out weeks ahead

why: Blue Lagoon's silica-milky water and black lava surround is genuinely unlike anywhere else on earth — a fitting final splurge on your last evening
Bridge Between Continents is a free 15-minute stop where you literally walk across the rift between the Eurasian and North American plates
Gunnuhver is Iceland's largest hot-spring mud-pool area — free, dramatically steamy at dusk, and 5 minutes from the van drop
tips: Van drop is at 09:30 Sat at Iðavellir 11, Keflavík — about 5 minutes' drive from the Blue Lagoon. Book a late Blue Lagoon slot (19:00–21:00) so you can do Gunnuhver and the Bridge in the afternoon without rushing, then soak at dusk

Fill up on groceries in Reykjavík or Hafnarfjörður on the way through — Keflavík options are limited and expensive. Cook your last van dinner at a Reykjanes campsite with geothermal views before the lagoon
days.cost: Blue Lagoon from ~10,990 ISK pp (Comfort entry) · Bridge free · Gunnuhver free · campsite ~4,000 ISK

stops.d: A footbridge spanning the Leif Eriksson Rift — the visible crack between the Eurasian and North American tectonic plates. It's genuinely surreal to stand in the gap. A certificate of crossing is available for a fee at the nearby gift hut, but the bridge itself is free and takes 15 minutes.
stops.cost: Free (certificate ~1,500 ISK if you want the souvenir)

stops.d: Iceland's most powerful geothermal area: thundering steam vents and grey mud pools that bubble and roar continuously. Named after a ghost said to have been lured into the boiling water by a priest. Boardwalk paths get you close — stay on them, the crust is dangerously thin. Completely free, open 24 hours.

The classic Iceland farewell: soak in 38–40°C silica-rich water surrounded by black lava as steam rises into the Arctic sky. Book the 19:00 slot for golden-hour light on the water. The white silica mud mask is included in all tiers — slather it on, it's part of the ritual.
stops.sp: Premium entry ~15,990 ISK pp — includes towel, robe, one drink at the lagoon bar
stops.bu: Grindavík village pool (Sundlaug Grindavíkur) ~900 ISK pp — geothermal, local, no queues

stops.d: Pick a campsite near Keflavík (Keflavík Campsite or Reykjanesbær) for the last night — you need to be within 10 minutes of Iðavellir 11 for the 09:30 drop. Cook your last van meal, open that bottle you've been saving, and watch the sun dip behind the Atlantic.

ideas.title: Landmannalaugar Highlands
ideas.desc: Rainbow rhyolite mountains — ochre, pink, green — surrounding a natural hot-spring river you wade right into. Requires a high-clearance 4WD on F-roads; not reachable in your 2WD campervan, so either join a day-tour from Vík or save it for a future trip.

ideas.title: Þórsmörk Valley
ideas.desc: A glacially carved valley between three glaciers — Eyjafjallajökull, Mýrdalsjökull and Tindfjallajökull — where birch forest clings to the ridges. Also F-road only for the final stretch; superjeep transfer from Seljalandsfoss is easy to book.

ideas.title: Fimmvörðuháls Pass Trek
ideas.desc: The legendary 25km one-way hike connecting Skógafoss to Þórsmörk, crossing the lava field born from the 2010 Eyjafjallajökull eruption. Take the bus back from Þórsmörk — one of the most varied day hikes in Europe.

ideas.title: Skaftafell Svartifoss Hike
ideas.desc: A 5km loop in Skaftafell to the basalt-column waterfall that inspired the columns in Reykjavík's Hallgrímskirkja — combine it with a glacier walk on Skaftafellsjökull right next door.

ideas.title: Kerlingarfjöll Hot Springs
ideas.desc: Fumaroles, steaming vents and boiling streams in a technicolour geothermal field deep in the highlands. F-road access (F35 Kjölur) is possible in a high-clearance 2WD with care in summer — check with your van hire first.

ideas.title: Mývatn Nature Baths
ideas.desc: The north's answer to the Blue Lagoon — milky-blue geothermal water on the shore of Mývatn at a fraction of the crowds and price. Locals say the silica here is even silkier; arrive after 8pm for the golden-hour steam.

ideas.title: Secret Lagoon (Gamla Laugin)
ideas.desc: Iceland's oldest swimming pool (1891), heated by a nearby geyser, ringed by wildflowers and trickling hot streams. Far quieter than the Blue Lagoon at a fraction of the cost — perfect end to the Golden Circle day.

ideas.title: Reykjadalur Hot River
ideas.desc: A 3km trail climbs gently to a steaming river you can actually bathe in — wade upstream to find your ideal temperature. Completely free, no booking, just bring a towel and go at sunrise to have it to yourself.

ideas.title: Jökulsárlón Glacier Lagoon
ideas.desc: Skyscraper-blue icebergs calve off Breiðamerkurjökull and drift slowly to the sea — a place that genuinely looks like another planet. Walk to Diamond Beach at the outlet to find crystal ice blocks scattered across black sand.

ideas.title: Westfjords & Látrabjarg Cliffs
ideas.desc: Europe's westernmost point and the largest seabird cliff in Iceland — puffins and razorbills perch within arm's reach at the cliff edge. A major detour from the Ring Road (allow 2–3 extra days) but nothing else in Iceland looks like this.

ideas.title: Dettifoss — Europe's Most Powerful Waterfall
ideas.desc: A wall of grey glacial water 45m high and 100m wide that physically shakes the ground underfoot — the raw power is genuinely shocking. The east-bank road (Route 864) gives the most dramatic close-up view.

ideas.title: Seljalandsfoss Walk-Behind
ideas.desc: The one waterfall you walk entirely behind — a narrow, slippery path circles behind the curtain for a dripping, theatrical view back out. Go in the evening when the golden-hour light turns the mist amber.

ideas.title: Vatnajökull National Park
ideas.desc: Europe's largest national park by area, covering a glacier bigger than all of mainland Europe's glaciers combined — Skaftafell, Jökulsárlón and Askja all sit within its boundaries.

ideas.title: Snæfellsjökull National Park
ideas.desc: Jules Verne's gateway to the centre of the Earth — a glacier-capped volcano at the tip of the peninsula, surrounded by lava fields, sea stacks and the coastal trail from Arnarstapi to Hellnar.

ideas.title: Northern Lights — Late August Odds
ideas.desc: Late August is the first realistic window — nights are dark enough by mid-August (astronomical twilight returns around 20 Aug), and solar activity in 2026 is near solar maximum. Best chances away from town on clear nights: Jökulsárlón, Mývatn, Snæfellsnes. No guarantees, but genuinely possible.

ideas.title: Húsavík Whale Watching
ideas.desc: Iceland's whale-watching capital — humpbacks and minkes feed close to shore all summer, and sightings run at about 95% in August. North Sailing runs oak-hulled schooners for a more atmospheric experience than the RIBs.

ideas.title: Ice Cave Tour (Crystal Cave)
ideas.desc: A natural ice cave inside Breiðamerkurjökull with walls of electric blue — only safely accessible from November through March as summer melt makes the ice unstable. Worth noting for a winter return, or book a summer 'lava tube' alternative through a local guide.

ideas.title: Puffin Spotting at Dyrhólaey
ideas.desc: Atlantic puffins nest on the sea stacks and clifftops here through late August — the Dyrhólaey lighthouse headland lets you watch them land and launch from just metres away. They typically depart early September, so late August is your last reliable chance.

ideas.title: Reykjavik Food Hall — Hlemmur Mathöll
ideas.desc: A converted 1930s bus terminal turned artisan food market — lamb soup, skyr cheesecake, fermented shark shots and craft beer under one roof. The best way to eat expensively in Reykjavík without feeling ripped off.

ideas.title: Icelandic Hot-Pot Etiquette
ideas.desc: Every town has a geothermal pool (sundlaug) — locals use them as community living rooms. Shower naked before entering (non-negotiable and enforced), speak quietly in the hot-pots, and never take photos inside. Entry costs 500–1200 ISK; bring shampoo.

checklists.title: 🎒 Iceland add-ons (on top of master packing)

items.t: Waterproof shell jacket + rain pants (wind-driven rain)
Sturdy hiking boots — REQUIRED for both glacier hikes
Sleep mask (bright until ~22:30 in late Aug)
Motion-sickness tablets for the whale boat
Cards only — Iceland is cashless, no ISK cash needed
Van pickup 13:00 Aug 20, Iðavellir 11 Keflavík — conf ICE00283; walk-around video + test stove/heater
Bónus stock-up right after pickup (cheapest chain)
Download offline: Google Maps Iceland + vedur.is app + safetravel.is alerts
Ask rental for N1/Orkan fuel discount fob
Vík + Skaftafell campsites = NO reservations, often full by 16:00 in Aug — arrive early those days
Van return 09:30 Aug 29 — fuel receipt from last Keflavík station + photos

bookings_to_make.book_by: NOW — book by 2026-07-15 (MANDATORY pre-book; evening slots gone 3–4wk ahead)
bookings_to_make.note: The classic farewell soak. Dynamic pricing — earlier = cheaper.

bookings_to_make.what: Sky Lagoon — Saman pass, Aug 20 evening
bookings_to_make.book_by: NOW — book by 2026-07-16 (Aug evenings sell out)
bookings_to_make.note: Saman = cheapest pass that includes the 7-step Skjól ritual.

bookings_to_make.book_by: NOW — book by 2026-07-17 (sells out weeks ahead)
bookings_to_make.note: Svínafellsjökull tours were DISCONTINUED — all Skaftafell hikes now run on Falljökull. Icelandic Mountain Guides, meet at Skaftafell booking centre.

bookings_to_make.book_by: NOW — book by 2026-07-17 (small boats, Aug sells out)
bookings_to_make.note: Zodiac 16,600 ISK pp (1h15) or Amphibian 7,100 ISK pp (40min, more capacity). Check-in 30min before.
Troll Expeditions 3h hike, from ~$107pp. Meet AT Sólheimajökull car park 20min early — pick the 13:30 departure.

bookings_to_make.what: Húsavík whale watching (North Sailing), Aug 26 AM
bookings_to_make.book_by: book by 2026-08-05 (Aug boats fill weeks out)
bookings_to_make.note: Original tour 12,990 ISK pp. Promo code SUMMER2026 = 10% off online. Free cancel to 24h.

bookings_to_make.what: Snæfellsjökull glacier, Aug 27 — DECIDE: summit hike vs ATV
